problem.title: Symmetric Tree
problem.description: Given the root of a binary tree, check whether it is a mirror of itself (i.e., symmetric around its center).

example.explanation: The tree is symmetric around its center.

solutions.title: Solution 1 – Recursive Approach
solutions.description: Two subtrees are mirrors of each other if their root values match and each subtree's left child mirrors the other's right child. Recursing on these cross-pairs simultaneously checks symmetry at every level in a single DFS pass. The key insight is rephrasing the single-tree symmetry question as a two-tree equality question with mirrored children.

complexity.space: O(h) where h is the height of the tree

solutions.title: Solution 2 – Iterative with Queue
solutions.description: A queue stores node pairs that must be mirrors of each other; pairs are dequeued, their values compared, and their children enqueued in mirrored order (left.left with right.right, left.right with right.left). The BFS continues until the queue is empty or a mismatch is found. This avoids recursion depth concerns for very tall trees while keeping O(n) time.
solutions.code_python:
# class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0): self.val=val; self.left=self.right=None
from collections import deque
from typing import Optional

def is_symmetric_iterative(root: Optional["TreeNode"]) -> bool:
    if root is None:
        return True

    queue: deque = deque([root.left, root.right])

    while queue:
        left = queue.popleft()
        right = queue.popleft()

        if left is None and right is None:
            continue
        if left is None or right is None:
            return False
        if left.val != right.val:
            return False

        queue.append(left.left)
        queue.append(right.right)
        queue.append(left.right)
        queue.append(right.left)

    return True

complexity.space: O(w) where w is the maximum width
